heading: TONUS SEXTUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the Sixth Tone of De Harmonia Mundi with its first chapter, titled "Homo Deum natura aemulatur" (Man emulates God by nature). Zorzi undertakes to explain the nature of the imago Dei in man, arguing that the commonplace Augustinian/scholastic identification of the image with the three faculties of the soul (memory, intellect, and will) is incomplete and insufficiently precise. He distinguishes between vestigium (a partial trace) and imago (a full representation) using standard scholastic terminology, then cites "sharper" more recent theologians who extend the image beyond the three first-act powers to include the second-act products: the verbum generated by intellect and the amor emanating from will — prefiguring the divine Word and Holy Spirit. The chapter sets the stage for a more expansive anthropology that situates man as a comprehensive icon of divinity beyond trinitarian analogy alone.
essay:
## Overview and Context

Chunk 96 marks the opening of the **Tonus Sextus** (Sixth Tone) of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*, the final major tonal division of the work. The heading of the first chapter announces its programme with classical concision: *Homo Deum natura aemulatur* — "Man emulates God by nature." The chapter is explicitly framed as a new departure: having established harmonic cosmology in earlier tones, Zorzi now turns to **theological anthropology**, investigating the manner in which the human being "agrees with God and is assimilated to Him" (*qua ratione homo cum Deo conveniat, et ipsi assimiletur*).

## The Question of the Imago Dei

Zorzi begins from the universally accepted scriptural datum — that man was made in the image (*imago*) and likeness (*similitudo*) of God (Genesis 1:26–27) — but immediately signals dissatisfaction with the received explanation:

> *Omnibus enim iam triviale est hominem cum imagine Dei factum, et ad ipsius deductum similitudinem. In quo autem consistat haec imago (ut cum bona venia dixerim eorum qui de hac scripserunt) cum adhuc non sit enucleatum, perscrutemur.*

<cite>p. 2302</cite>

The irenic but pointed phrase *cum bona venia dixerim eorum qui de hac scripserunt* — "with due respect to those who have written on this" — signals a critical move: Zorzi is not rejecting tradition but refining it. The question of *in quo consistat haec imago* (wherein this image consists) has not yet been properly "enucleated" (enucleatum — stripped to its kernel), he argues.

## Vestigium vs. Imago: The Scholastic Distinction

Zorzi invokes a technical scholastic distinction between **vestigium** (trace/footprint) and **imago** (image proper). The distinction goes back to Augustine (*De Trinitate*) and was elaborated by Thomas Aquinas and Bonaventure: an *imago* represents the whole and every aspect of its original (*repraesentare totum, et omnimodum effigiatum*), while a *vestigium* represents only some ratio or part of the original. He attributes this precision to "our more perspicacious theologians" (*a theologis nostris perspicacioribus quam optime decernitur*).

The standard teaching — that the three divine Persons are represented in the three powers of the soul: **memory, intellect, and will** (*memoria, intellectus, voluntas*) — Zorzi dismisses as *vulgari et anili doctrina* ("commonplace and old-wifely doctrine"). This is a deliberately provocative characterisation: the trinitarian-psychological analogy is of course Augustinian in origin and central to scholastic theology. Zorzi's objection is not that it is false, but that it is incomplete — and that, taken alone, it can only qualify as a *vestigium*, not a true *imago*.

## The "Sharper" Recent Theologians: Second-Act Powers

The chapter pivots to what Zorzi calls *novitioribus theologis aliqui acutiores* — "certain sharper among the more recent theologians" — who move beyond the three powers as *actus primi* (first acts, i.e., the faculties themselves) to include their **actus secundi** (second acts, i.e., the operations and products of those faculties):

> *Est enim in nobis intellectus, memoria, atque voluntas, haecque eadem tria in Deo repraesentant. Est insuper verbum ab intellectu nostro productum, et amor a voluntate emanans, quorum altero divinum verbum, altero autem Spiritus sanctus...*

<cite>p. 2302–2303</cite>

This is a significant move in Zorzi's anthropology. The soul not only *has* the three faculties (first acts) that mirror the Trinity, but it *generates* a **verbum** (word/concept) from intellect and an **amor** (love) emanating from will — each mirroring the procession of the divine Word (the Son/Logos) and the Holy Spirit respectively. The inclusion of second-act products ties human cognitive and affective life directly into a dynamic, processive image of the Trinity, rather than a merely structural one.

## Theological and Philosophical Significance

This chapter opens a project that will occupy much of the Sixth Tone: establishing man not merely as a trinitarian analogue but as a **plenissima repraesentatio** of the whole divine nature. The phrase *plura tamen in Deo explicari possunt, quae homo plenissime repraesentat in aliis partibus, quam in praedictis tribus potentiis* signals that Zorzi's anthropology is not simply Augustinian or Thomistic — it is expansive, drawing on Neoplatonic emanationism and, as the Tone will develop, on Kabbalistic doctrines of the Sefirot as a fuller map of the divine attributes mirrored in man.

The Neoplatonic background is audible in the language of *emanans* (emanating) for the production of love from will — a term with strong Plotinian resonance — and in the dynamic, processual model of mind that Zorzi is constructing.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

The Sixth Tone's concern with the *homo-Deus* relationship crowns the harmonic cosmology of the preceding tones: if the world is ordered by divine harmony, man — as the image of God — is the microcosm in whom that harmony is most fully instantiated. The chapter's opening insistence that the *imago Dei* has not yet been adequately explained sets Zorzi's programme in relief against the scholastic tradition, positioning his synthesis of Augustinian psychology, Neoplatonic emanationism, and (forthcoming) Kabbalistic symbolism as the fuller account that tradition has been approaching but not reaching.

The Italian section heading preserved in the chunk — *Sull'accordo tra l'uomo e Dio, perché l'uno possa godere dell'altro* ("On the agreement between man and God, so that the one may enjoy the other") — underlines the telos of the whole investigation: not merely speculative theology but a contemplative and participatory vision of human destiny in union with the divine.
